title: 3  Homework 2: Data Plots on Measures of Performance
text:
This Chapter will focus on the topics surrounding Visualizing Data Models
Topics includes:

Discrimination (Somer’s D/C-index)
Calibration plot
Residual plots
Proper scoring rules
Expected squared error
Expected absolute error
Error distribution Stratification across subgroups


4 Module 2 homework

In your repo, create the HW2.qmd and add it to your book (_quarto.yml).
For each prediction rule, create two calibration curves: one for x3=0 and another for x3=1.



5 Installing the properties and packages if needed:


6 ```{r}
#install.packages(“pak”) #pak::pak(“thomasgstewart/ds6021b”) #pak::pak(“thomasgstewart/ds6021c”) library(ds6021b) library(ds6021c)
set.seed(23583) data_for_predictionModel1 <- external_data(n=100)


7 All prediction models for all prediction rules and create two calibration curves: one for x3=0 and another for x3=1.
data <- external_data(1000)


8 calibration curve for x3=0 and x3=1
x3_0 <- data\(x3 == 0
x3_1 <- data\)x3 == 1 # Create side-by-side plotting area par(mfrow = c(1,2)) # Loop through each prediction rule and create calibration curves for x3=0 and x3=1 for (fi in c(“f1”, “f2”, “f3”, “f4”, “f5”)) { # Create calibration curve for x3=0 plot(get(fi)(data[x3_0, ]), data[x3_0, ]\(y,
         xlab = paste("Predicted", fi, " values"),
         ylab = paste("Observed Y values from the Predicted ", fi, " values"),
         main = paste("Prediction Rule Model: ", fi, "at (x3=0)"),
         col = "#aa4848", type = "p")
    lines(lowess(get(fi)(data[x3_0, ]), data[x3_0, ]\)y), col = “black”) abline(0, 1, lty = 2, lwd = 2, col = “blue”) # calibration curve for x3=1 plot(get(fi)(data[x3_1, ]), data[x3_1, ]\(y,
         xlab = paste("Predicted", fi, " values"),
         ylab = paste("Observed Y values from the Predicted ", fi, " values"),
         main = paste("Prediction Rule Model: ", fi, " at (x3=1)"),
         col = "#aa4848", type = "p")
    lines(lowess(get(fi)(data[x3_1, ]), data[x3_1, ]\)y), col = “black”) abline(0, 1, lty = 2, lwd = 2, col = “darkgreen”) }

For each prediction rule, create a visualization of the residual distribution. for (fi in c(“f1”,“f2”, “f3”, “f4”, “f5”)) { resid_value <- get(fi)(data) - data$y hist(resid_value, freq = FALSE, main = paste(“Residuals model values for the Prediction Rule Model:”, fi), xlab = paste(“Residual values (predicted value (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5) of y - observed value of y)”), ylab = paste(“Probabilities of the Y values from the Predicted”, fi, ” values”), col = “#aa4848”, border = “black”, breaks = 30) # Plot the density curve lines(density(resid_value), col = “blue”) }
For each prediction rule, calculate expected squared error, expected absolute error, and the 90th quantile. Place the calculated quantities in a visualization for easy comparison. (You may want to create a different visualization for each summary measure.)



9 Expected squared error
mean_squared_error_vals <- sapply(c(“f1”,“f2”,“f3”,“f4”,“f5”), function(fi) { mean((get(fi)(data) - data$y)^2) })
plot(1:5, mean_squared_error_vals, xlab = “Prediction Rules (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5)”, ylab = “Mean Squared Error for each Prediction Rule Model”, main = “Expected Squared Error by each Prediction Rule Model”, col = “#0c27bf”, ylim = c(0,max(mean_squared_error_vals)), pch = 16, cex = 1.5, xaxt = “n”) axis(1, 1:5, c(“f1”,“f2”,“f3”,“f4”,“f5”))


10 Expected absolute error
mae_vals <- sapply(c(“f1”,“f2”,“f3”,“f4”,“f5”), function(fi) { mean(abs(get(fi)(data) - data$y)) })
plot(1:5, mae_vals, xlab = “Prediction Rules (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5)”, ylab = “Mean Absolute Error for each Prediction Rule Model”, main = “Expected Absolute Error by each Prediction Rule Model”, col = “#098001”, ylim = c(0,max(mae_vals)), pch = 16, cex = 1.5, xaxt = “n”) axis(1, 1:5, c(“f1”,“f2”,“f3”,“f4”,“f5”)) # 90th quantile quantile_90th_vals <- sapply(c(“f1”,“f2”,“f3”,“f4”,“f5”), function(fi) { quantile(abs(get(fi)(data) - data$y), 0.9) })
plot(1:5, quantile_90th_vals, xlab = “Prediction Rules (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5)”, ylab = “90th Quantile for each Prediction Rule Model”, main = “90th Quantile by each Prediction Rule Model”, col = “#ff0000”, ylim = c(0,max(quantile_90th_vals)), pch = 16, cex = 1.5, xaxt = “n”) axis(1, 1:5, c(“f1”,“f2”,“f3”,“f4”,“f5”)) # ```

Describe a measure of model performance from the other section.

After going through all of the sections of this homework assignment, I found that each graph and predictual rule model revealed some unique details and insights about the measure of performances and how these models are used to help make accurate predictions and decions from these models.
Starting with the calibration curves, these curves are used to evaluate the performance of a Predictive model by comparing the Predicted (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5) probabilities of all x related data whose data that matches the x3 values of 0 and 1 to the observed Observed Y values from the Predicted (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5) values. The first solid line of the calibration curves for x3=0 along with x3=1 helped to showed how the data points were correlated in a smoother linear way to be represent the relationship between the predicted probabilities and the observed frequencies. While the second dashed line in the calibration curves for x3=0 along with x3=1 best represents the reference line of the calibration curve that helps to represent the ideal straight linear relationship between the predicted probabilities and the observed frequencies. Altogether, these calibration curves for x3=0 along with x3=1 helps us better understand how well the predicted probabilities from the model align with the actual observed outcomes to see which relationships in the data better represents the ideal calibration of how data is predicted and observed in the models. From here, we can make better decisions based on the accurate prediciton calibrations of the models and we could also use these models to judge the performances of data in models in a precise and calibrated way.
Going on into the residual distribution visualization, this histogram model helps us understand the distribution of residuals, which are the differences between the predicted values from the models and the observed values specified in the y variable in the data. After graphing this histogram, we can see how well the model fits the data into an almost perfect bell curve distribution of the residuals which helps to conclude that the model’s predictions are well fitted to the observed data since the highest frequency of residual values are clustered around zero. This also means that this model’s predictions are most perfectly the same value as the observed y values of the data. If the model was skewed or sparsely distributed into disjointed clusters, then the data of the residuals would not be well fitted to the observed data and this would lead to poor performance and inaccurate assessments of the data. It is also important to note that the density curve of the residuals that I added helps reinforce the accuracy and reliability of the model’s predictions by showing the overall shape of the residual distribution. Altogether, this histogram of residuals for each prediction rule model (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5) helps us understand how well the model fits the data and whether there are any patterns or biases in the predictions along with the data. On top of that, this histogram of residuals for each prediction rule model (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5) helps us finalize that data predictions could be relied upon in decision making surrounding the precise calibration of the data in its later usage and how it does not deviate away from any previous observed data values.
Finally for the measure of performances of calculate expected squared error, expected absolute error, and the 90th quantile, anyone could see by these visualizations that the reliability of the data predictions of these models could be used to determine accuracy and validity of the data. For example, within the Expected Squared Error visualization, we can see that the model reveals that all of the squared error mean values of each prediction rule (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5) are relatively close to each other since the cover in the 1 probability range of the y axis. However, the model with the lowest mean squared error is the most accurate model in terms of its predictions being close to the observed values on the y axis. It is important to know that there is one mean squared error value in the prediction rule f1 that is the highest which would indicate some potential biased and inaccurates prediction values to the other prediction rules along with being slightly less accurate than the other prediction rules. Regardless though, the mean squared error values reveals that all of the predictions are close in value to their observed values and thus are closely accurate predictions along with having a linear relationship regardless if there is no relationship line to connect them together. When viewing the expected absolute error rate visualization, we can seem a similar linear pattern in the data where all of the prediction values of each prediction rule (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5) are relatively close to each other since the cover in the 0.8 probability range of the y axis. Thus, it would be easy for anyone to conclude that all of the prediction results of the predicted value to the observed data values are relatively close to each other and thus are closely accurate predictions. Finally for the visualization describing the 90th quantile, we can see that all of the prediction values of each prediction rule (f1, f2, f3, f4, f5) are relatively close to each other since the cover in the 1.5 probability range of the y axis. Thus, it would be easy for anyone to conclude that all of the prediction results of the predicted value to the observed data values are relatively close to each other and thus are closely accurate predictions.
Altogether, these visualizations of expected squared error, expected absolute error, and the 90th quantile help us understand which models are more accurate and reliable in their predictions and which models may be less accurate and reliable. This information can be used to make informed decisions about which models to use for future decisions, predictions and analysis in comparison to other models.

title: 4  Module 3 homework: Internal measures of performance
text:
This Chapter will focus on the topics surrounding Internal measures of performance
Topics includes:

Apparent error
Estimating the error rate and its uncertainty
K-fold CV – Bootstrap – 0.632 & 0.632+
Optimism


5 Module 3 homework
Module 3 homework 1. In your repo, create HW3.qmd and add it to your book (_quarto.yml). 2. Calculate the apparent MAE and external MAE for all 100 models in m using their corresponding training datasets in td. Create a scatter plot with apparent MAE on the x-axis and external MAE on the y-axis. Use a large external dataset to calculate MAE. Describe what you observe.


6 Installing the properties and packages if needed:


7 ```{r}
install.packages(“pak”, quiet = TRUE) pak::pak(“thomasgstewart/ds6021d”) library(ds6021d)


8 2.) Calculate the apparent MAE and external MAE for all 100 models in m


9 using their corresponding training datasets in td.


10 Create a scatter plot with apparent MAE on the x-axis and external MAE on


11 the y-axis. Use a large external dataset to calculate MAE.


12 Describe what you observe.
vector_for_apparent_MAE_100_models <- rep(NA, 100) vectors_for_external_MAE_100_models <- rep(NA, 100) set.seed(2343)
for(index in seq_along(vector_for_apparent_MAE_100_models)){ m_functions <- m[[index]] td_internal_training_data <- td[[index]]
td_internal_training_predition_data_funct_y_out <- m_functions(td_internal_training_data)
td_internal_training_y_data <- td_internal_training_data$y
apparent_rate_MAE <- mean(abs(td_internal_training_predition_data_funct_y_out - td_internal_training_y_data))
vector_for_apparent_MAE_100_models[index] <- apparent_rate_MAE

external_data_vals <- external_data(10000)  # larger external set
external_data_vals_funct_y_out <- m_functions(external_data_vals)
external_rate_MAE <- mean(abs(external_data_vals_funct_y_out - external_data_vals$y))
vectors_for_external_MAE_100_models[index] <- external_rate_MAE
}
plot(x = vector_for_apparent_MAE_100_models, y = vectors_for_external_MAE_100_models, main = “Apparent MAE vs External MAE”, xlab = “Apparent MAE (Training)”, ylab = “External MAE (Test)”, pch = 19, col = “steelblue”, cex = 1.2)


13 3.) For each of the 100 models, compute the optimism


14 for MAE (apparent MAE minus external MAE).


15 Plot the distribution of optimism.


16 Calculate the mean optimism for MAE.


17 Is the distribution roughly symmetric or skewed?


18 What does the figure tell you about using apparent error


19 as a performance estimate?
vector_for_optimism_MAE_100_models <- rep(NA, 100) for(index in seq_along(vector_for_apparent_MAE_100_models)){ vector_for_optimism_MAE_100_models[index] <- vector_for_apparent_MAE_100_models[index] - vectors_for_external_MAE_100_models[index] } mean_optimism_for_MAE_100_models <- mean(vector_for_optimism_MAE_100_models)
hist(vector_for_optimism_MAE_100_models, main = “Distribution of Optimism (Apparent MAE - External MAE)”, xlab = “Optimism for MAE”, col = “lightblue”, border = “darkblue”) abline(v = mean_optimism_for_MAE_100_models, col = “red”, lwd = 2) text(x = mean_optimism_for_MAE_100_models, y = -0.5, labels = paste(“Mean =”, round(mean_optimism_for_MAE_100_models, 5)), col = “red”)


20 4.) Select td[[1]] as your working dataset.


21 Implement 10-fold CV for the regression model


22 in the section 3 examples and record the resulting


23 MAE estimate.
new_train_data <- td[[1]] number_of_rows_training_data <- nrow(new_train_data) k_val <- 10 set.seed(123) folds_randomized <- sample(rep(1:k_val, length.out = number_of_rows_training_data))
cv_mae <- rep(NA, k_val) for(k in 1:k_val){ val_idx_of_CV <- which(folds_randomized == k) train_idx_of_CV <- which(folds_randomized != k)
train_fold_of_CV <- new_train_data[train_idx_of_CV, ]
val_fold_of_CV <- new_train_data[val_idx_of_CV, ]

linear_model_of_fold_of_CV <- lm(y ~ ., data = train_fold_of_CV)
predict_y_val_of_fold_of_CV <- predict(linear_model_of_fold_of_CV, newdata = val_fold_of_CV)

cv_mae[k] <- mean(abs(predict_y_val_of_fold_of_CV - val_fold_of_CV$y))

cat("10-fold CV for the regression model: Fold number: ", k, ", MAE =", round(cv_mae[k], 4), "\n")
}


24 mean of 10-fold CV for MAE
mean_of_10_fold_CV_for_MAE <- mean(cv_mae) print(mean_of_10_fold_CV_for_MAE)


25 5.) Using td[[1]], implement the bootstrap optimism estimator with B=200


26 bootstrap samples. Report the apparent MAE, the estimated optimism,


27 and the corrected MAE.


28 Compare the corrected MAE to the true external error (computed with a large external dataset).
b_val <- 200 set.seed(42352) optimism_b <- rep(NA, b_val)
m_function <- m[[1]] new_training_predition_data_funct_y_output <- m_function(new_train_data)
apparent_error_mae <- mean(abs(new_training_predition_data_funct_y_output - new_train_data$y)) # using original m[[1]]
for (b in 1:b_val){ idx_training_data <- sample(1:number_of_rows_training_data, replace = TRUE) bootstrap_training_data <- new_train_data[idx_training_data, ]
linearly_fittted_model_b_value <- lm(y ~ ., data = bootstrap_training_data)

# In-sample on bootstrap
predicted_y_value_b_value_training_data_bootstrap_in_sample <- predict(linearly_fittted_model_b_value, newdata = bootstrap_training_data)
mae_optimism_in_sample_b_value <- mean(abs(predicted_y_value_b_value_training_data_bootstrap_in_sample - bootstrap_training_data$y))

# original training data
predicted_y_value_b_value_training_data_bootstrap_original <- predict(linearly_fittted_model_b_value, newdata = new_train_data)
mae_optimism_original_b_value <- mean(abs(predicted_y_value_b_value_training_data_bootstrap_original - new_train_data$y))

# optimism for bootstrap formula
optimism_b[b] <- mae_optimism_in_sample_b_value - mae_optimism_original_b_value
}


29 corrected optimism bootstrap MAE
bootstrap_corrected_mae <- apparent_error_mae - mean(optimism_b)


30 True external error
large_external <- external_data(20000) true_external_error_mae <- mean(abs(predict(lm(y ~ ., data = new_train_data), newdata = large_external) - large_external$y))
cat(“Apparent MAE:”, round(apparent_error_mae, 5), “”) cat(“Bootstrap optimism:”, round(mean(optimism_b), 5), “”) cat(“Corrected MAE:”, round(bootstrap_corrected_mae, 5), “”) cat(“True external MAE:”, round(true_external_error_mae, 5), “”)
hist(optimism_b, main = “Bootstrap estimates of optimism”, xlab = “Optimism (in-sample MAE − original MAE)”, freq = FALSE) abline(v = mean(optimism_b), col = “blue”, lwd = 2) text(x = mean(optimism_b), y = 0, labels = paste(“Mean =”, round(mean_optimism, 5)), col = “blue”) # ```

title: 5  Name: Maxwell Braseth
text:
6 Date: 06/14/2026


7 Module 4 Homework


8 ```{r}
install.packages(“plotly”) install.packages(“dplyr”) install.packages(“ggplot2”) library(plotly) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) set.seed(42)


9 1.)


10 In your repo, create the HW4.qmd and add it to your book


11 (_quarto.yml).


12 2.)


13 Suppose X1 has 4 categories,


14 X2 has 6 categories, and


15 X3 has 3 categories.


16 X1 ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4} –> 4 categories


17 X2 ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} –> 6 categories


18 X3 ∈ {1, 2, 3} –> 3 categories


19 X1 * X2 * X3 = 4 * 6 * 3 = 72 cells
x1 <- c(1, 2, 3, 4) x2 <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) x3 <- c(1, 2, 3)


20 How many cells does the largest possible model contain?
total_cells <- (length(x1) * length(x2) * length(x3)) # (x1 * x2 * x3) print(paste(“The maximum number of cells within the model is:”, total_cells)) print(paste(“This is because the largest possible model (the saturated model) requires a unique cell for every possible joint combination of the categories, calculated as 4 × 6 × 3 = 72 cells.”)) print(paste(“These 72 cells represent the complete classification of all three covariates, spanning from (1, 1, 1) through (4, 6, 3), allowing the model to capture all of the independently predicted values of the combined covariate cell values.”))


21 How many parameters does the smallest possible model require?
parameters_for_smallest_model <- 1 print(paste(“The minimum number of parameters within the model is:”, parameters_for_smallest_model)) print(paste(“This is because the smallest possible model (the intercept-only model) which ignores all covariate information and assigns the same prediction value”, “to every observation regardless of the combinations of the values of X1, X2, and X3.”)) print(paste(“At the same time, this model needs a intercept constant value that provides the same prediction value regardless of the combinations of the covariates values of x1, x2, and x3. This also helps estimate the mean of this smallest model.”))


22 If a training dataset of n=30 observations is drawn uniformly at


23 random from this covariate space,


24 roughly how many cells would you expect to be unobserved?


25 Set parameters
set.seed(42) n <- 30


26 Mathematical Formula for the Expected Number of Unobserved Cells


27 E(cells_unobserved = UC) = total_cells = M * (1 - 1 / total_cells + M)^n
cells_unobserved_expected <- total_cells * (1 - 1 / total_cells)^n print(paste(“Theoretical expected unobserved cells:”, round(cells_unobserved_expected, 0)))


28 graph explanation to the observed and unobserved cells


29 Continuous Uniform Simulation


30 uniformly between 0 and 1 for n=30 observations for x, y, and z
idx_x1 <- sample(x1, n, replace = TRUE) idx_x2 <- sample(x2, n, replace = TRUE) idx_x3 <- sample(x3, n, replace = TRUE) # creating the cell matrix/grid grid_matrix <- expand.grid( x1 = x1, x2 = x2, x3 = x3 ) # Initialize observation count grid_matrix\(Obs_Count <- 0
# Looping throught the total n observations and filling the matrix/grid cells
for(i in 1:n){
  matched_row <- which(
    grid_matrix\)x1 == idx_x1[i] & grid_matrix\(x2 == idx_x2[i] &
    grid_matrix\)x3 == idx_x3[i] ) # incrementing the observation count of matched values the covariates of x1=4, x2=6, and x3=3 grid_matrix\(Obs_Count[matched_row] <-
    grid_matrix\)Obs_Count[matched_row] + 1 }


31 Number of unobserved cells and observed cells
simulated_unobserved <- sum(grid_matrix\(Obs_Count == 0)
simulated_observed <- sum(grid_matrix\)Obs_Count > 0)
print(paste(“Simulated unobserved cells:”, simulated_unobserved)) print(paste(“Simulated observed cells:”, simulated_observed)) print(paste(“Difference between simulated and expected unobserved cells:”,round(simulated_unobserved - cells_unobserved_expected, 0)))


32 3.)


33 Using the linear interpolation model, calculate the prediction ŷ(X0 = 0.45)


34 Wa = (Xb - X0) / (Xb - Xa)


35 Wb = (X0 - Xa) / (Xb - Xa)


36 ŷ(X0) = Wa * ya + Wb * yb


37 given training points and the observed value of x0: x0=0.45, xa = 0.38, ya = 1.92, xb = 0.57, yb = 2.04.
x0 <- 0.45 ya <- 1.92 xa <- 0.38 xb <- 0.57 yb <- 2.04 # Show the weights Wa and Wb wa <- (xb - x0) / (xb - xa) wb <- (x0 - xa) / (xb - xa) # setting the value of ŷ(X0) prediction_y_x0 <- wa * ya + wb * yb # verifying that the weights of Wa and Wb sum to 1 print(paste(“The sum of the weights is:”, wa + wb)) print(paste(“Difference between the prediction value of ŷ(X0) and the total weights of wa & wb:”, round(prediction_y_x0 - (wa + wb), 0)))


38 what is the relative magnitudes of the weights, tell you about how much


39 each neighbor contributes to the prediction.


40 given that the neighbors are considered to be the two training observations that help show the prediction of ŷ(X0)


41 and the neighbors are the left neighbor: wa=(Xa = 0.38, ya = 1.92) and the right neighbor: wb=(Xb = 0.57, yb = 2.04).


42 At the same time, these neighbords are closets values to the observed value of x0 to the left and right of this observed value.


43 relative magnitude(s) of the weights
print(paste(“Relative magnitude ratio for wa/wb:”, round(wa/wb), 2)) print(paste(“Relative magnitude ratio for wb/wa:”, round(wb/wa), 2)) print(paste(“Relative magnitude of the left neighbor:”, round(wa / (wa + wb), 2))) print(paste(“Relative magnitude of the right neighbor:”, round(wb / (wa + wb), 2))) print(paste(“Difference between the relative magnitude of the left neighbor and the right neighbor:”, round(wa / (wa + wb) - wb / (wa + wb), 0))) print(paste(“Is the left or the right neighbor larger or smaller?:”, “The left neighbor is larger than the right neighbor?:”, wa > wb, ” The right neighbor is larger than the left neighbor?: “, wb > wa)) if(wa > wb){ print(paste(”The left neighbor is larger than the right neighbor and this means that the relative magnitude of the left neighbor contributes more to the prediction of ŷ(X0) at a wa value of : “, round(wa, 2) * 100,”%“)) print(paste(”This also means that the left neighbor wa is closer to the observed value of x0 than the right neighbor wb.”)) print(paste(“Which also means that this linear interpolation model is better at predicting ŷ(X0) at a wa value of :”, round(wa, 2) * 100, “%”)) }else{ print(paste(“The right neighbor is larger than the left neighbor and this means that the relative magnitude of the right neighbor contributes more to the prediction of ŷ(X0) at a wb value of :”, round(wb, 2) * 100, “%”)) print(paste(“This also means that the right neighbor wb is closer to the observed value of x0 than the left neighbor wa.”)) print(paste(“Which also means that this linear interpolation model is better at predicting ŷ(X0) at a wb value of :”, round(wb, 2) * 100, “%”)) }


44 4.) Using the following data, calculate the 3 nearest neigbhor prediction


45 for X0 = 0.2, 0.5, 0.7 by hand.


46 x y


47 0.22 2.14


48 0.37 1.89


49 0.57 1.99


50 0.66 1.88


51 0.03 2.26


52 0.92 2.11


53 0.77 1.92


54 0.73 1.98


55 0.11 2.47


56 0.40 2.10


57 Before selecting the 3 closest k=3 neighbors, measuring the distance between the new observed point (x) and a known training point (y) across (n) different features.


58 Distance Metrics Formulas:


59 Euclidean Distance: Σ(√(xi-yi)^2) - yi : sqrt((x2 - x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2)


60 Euclidean Distance in this context: Σ(x0-xi) - yi : |x2 - x1|


61 Manhatten Distance: Σ|xi - yi| - yi : |x2 - x1| + |y2 - y1|


62 3 nearest neighbors for x0 = 0.2


63 3 nearest neighbors for x0 = 0.5


64 3 nearest neighbors for x0 = 0.7


65 where x0 = 0.2


66 f(xi) = Σ(x0-xi)


67 f(x=0.22) = 0.20 - 0.22 = |-0.02| = 0.02 –> y = 2.14


68 f(x=0.37) = 0.20 - 0.37 = |-0.17| = 0.17 –> y = 1.89


69 f(x=0.57) = 0.20 - 0.57 = |-0.37| = 0.37 –> y = 1.99


70 f(x=0.66) = 0.20 - 0.66 = |-0.54| = 0.54 –> y = 1.88


71 f(x=0.03) = 0.20 - 0.03 = |0.17| = 0.17 –> y = 2.26


72 f(x=0.92) = 0.20 - 0.92 = |-0.72| = 0.72 –> y = 2.11

#6.) In your own words, explain why the “largest model” # — which assigns an independent estimate to every covariate # combination — can be a poor choice in practice even though it # fits the training data perfectly. # Connect your answer to the concept of information sharing introduced # in the chapter, and describe what characteristic of the data-generating # process would make the largest model a reasonable choice.


129 When considering the topic of why the “Largest Model” independently predicts and estimates the outcome for each cell in the combinations of covariates, it is important to recognize that the model assigns a separate prediction to every possible combination of covariate values (for example, x1 = 4 categories, x2 = 6 categories, and x3 = 3 categories, resulting in 72 possible cells).


130 The largest model can be a poor choice in practice because it simply tracks the mean outcome within each observed cell and does not share information across cells. While this allows the model to fit the training data perfectly, it does not provide a reliable way to make predictions for cells that are unobserved or contain very few observations. If some covariate combinations are not represented in the training data, then no cell-specific mean can be calculated for those combinations.


131 This lack of information sharing can result in overfitting. The model may appear to perform extremely well on the observed training data because each cell is estimated independently, but its predictions may not generalize well to new data. In other words, the apparent error on the training data may be very small, while the prediction error on future observations may be much larger.


132 When considering the concept of Information Sharing in this context, information sharing describes how observations contribute to predictions beyond their own cell. The best models often lie between the largest model (no information sharing) and the smallest model (maximum information sharing). These models allow observations to influence predictions in neighboring cells or groups, either locally (such as nearest-neighbor methods) or globally (such as regression models).


133 Keeping this in mind, the largest model would be considered reasonable if the data-generating process truly produced independent outcomes for every covariate combination and there were sufficient observations within every cell to estimate the cell-specific mean accurately. In that situation, little or no information sharing would be needed because each cell could be reliably estimated using its own observed data.


134 Finally, the largest model would be acceptable when all covariate combinations are well represented in the dataset and each cell contains enough observations to produce stable predictions. Under those conditions, the model could accurately describe the data-generating process without needing to borrow information from neighboring cells or groups.


135 ```

35 3. Written reflection: Which model performs best on the Abalone data,


36 and which definition of best are you using to decide? What are some of the


37 different considerations that might lead to different choices about which prediction model to use?


38 Given the MSE and Calibration plots values and conclusions, it was clear to mean and to others


39 that the Random Forest model performed the best in terms of predicting the age of the Abalone data in cross comparison to the actual


40 observations of the age of the Abalone witihin the data. This is evidenced by the fact that the Random Forest model had the lowest


41 RMSE values for both the training and testing data which means that the predictions were very close to the actual observations


42 in terms of all of the non-linear relationships of the Abalone rings(age) values with all of the variables values of the data (both numeric and non-numeric).


43 This conclusions that the Random Forest model performing the best in term of predictions


44 also helped enforce my definition that it was the best model to use due to how the Random Forest model was able to efficiently predict the age of the Abalone data and is very


45 scalable and adaptable to new testing data and even with predicting the outcome values given the actual training data observations.


46 It is even important to note that the calibration plot of the Random Forest model showed the predictions were close in distance from the actual observations of the data.


47 Which this meant that the Random Forest model prediction ring(age) values/counts were very close to the actual observations of the data and the model’s predictions matched


48 the identity line of the calibration plot that means that seems to fit the model perfectly along with avoiding over and under fitting when dealing with new data.


49 However, it is important to note that the best model in terms of accurate predictions would need to be a model that is robust and able to keep accurate predictions in the face of new testing data.


50 In this case of the 70-30 split of the data and considering the robustness of the models, Random Forest Models would actually not be the best model since when I altered the data or entered new data the predictions dramatically shifted.


51 As a result of this reality, the next models that I would consider to be a good model for prediction would be the Oridinal Regression and the Linear Regression (OLS + RCS) models.


52 Within those models, the Oridinal Regression model performed the best in terms of accurate predictions through its RMSE calculations and the Linear Regression (OLS + RCS) model performed the best in terms of interpretability since its predictors had


53 a deep influence on its calibration plot outputs (in other words its predictor variables gave the expected accurate predictions in term of the number of rings of the Abalone data and they were accurate against the training data in the model).


54 It is also important to note that when I tested these models on the training data, the Oridinal Regression model performed the best and showed adaptability when confronted when I ran the test data against this model which showed a semi-good performance in dealing with over and underfitting.


55 At the same time, the Linear Regression (OLS + RCS) model performed better than the Ordinal Regression Model in terms of its calibration models which means that the predictions of the regression actually closely matched the observed ring(age) values/counts of the Abalone data.


56 In conclusion, the best ways that I would describe a good prediction model is that it does need a lower MSE and RMSE amount and needs a plot such as a calibration plot that reinforces the conclusions that the predictions ring(age) values/counts are close to the actual observations of the data,


57 while still being adaptable to new testing data and out of sample data to be considered an efficient and robust prediction model.

# Loading the insurance.csv data
insurance_data <- read.csv("data/insurance.csv", encoding = "UTF-8")
library(MASS)
library(rms)

Loading required package: Hmisc



Attaching package: 'Hmisc'


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    format.pval, units

# 1.) Write down the linear regression model 
# mathematically. Use matrix notation Y = Xβ + ε.
# Identify each component: what is Y, what 
# columns are in X, what does each element of β
# represent?

# Y = Xβ + ε
# Y = dependent variable --> response or obsered dependent response variable
# X = independent variable --> Design matrix
# β = coefficient --> (X^T X)^-1 X^T Y
# X^T = transpose of the matrix
# (X^T X)^-1 --> inverse of the matrix product X^T * X
#   * example of an inverse of a matrix: a^-1 = 1/a --> a = [a, b, c, d] and a^-1 = [1/a, 1/b, 1/c, 1/d] or A^-1 = 1/|A| * Adj(A) = 1/(ad-bc) * [d, -b, -c, a]
# ε = error -- > # ε = Y - Xβ = ŷ - y
# ŷ = predicted value of y = Xβ

# Set factor levels
insurance_data$sex    <- factor(insurance_data$sex,    levels = c("female", "male"))
insurance_data$region <- factor(insurance_data$region, levels = c("northeast", "northwest", "southeast", "southwest"))
insurance_data$smoker <- factor(insurance_data$smoker, levels = c("no", "yes"))

# creating design matrices to combine together all of the independent predictor variables of x
sex_matrix    <- model.matrix(~ 0 + sex,    data = insurance_data)
region_matrix <- model.matrix(~ 0 + region, data = insurance_data)
smoker_matrix <- model.matrix(~ 0 + smoker, data = insurance_data)
age_matrix     <- as.matrix(insurance_data$age)
bmi_matrix     <- as.matrix(insurance_data$bmi)
children_matrix <- as.matrix(insurance_data$children)

# Combine all of the x variables within matrices format into full design matrix
# what are columns are in X and what does they represent?
# These x variables are the independent predictor variables that influences the linear regression relationship of showing how 
# the features of individuals (such as age, sex, bmi, number of children, etc...) affect the cost of health insurance charges in a positive or negative way.
x <- cbind(
  sex_matrix, 
  region_matrix, 
  smoker_matrix, 
  age_matrix, 
  bmi_matrix, 
  children_matrix
)

# Give clear column names
colnames(x) <- c(
  "sexfemale", "sexmale",
  "regionnortheast", "regionnorthwest", "regionsoutheast", "regionsouthwest",
  "smokerno", "smokeryes",
  "age", "bmi", "children"
)
# Y dependent variable for all x independent variables
y <- insurance_data$charges

# Use generalized inverse for the Coefficient Estimate equation (X^T * X)^-1 * X^T * Y
# (X^T X)^-1 X^T Y
b <- ginv(t(x) %*% x) %*% t(x) %*% y

# assigning the column and row names
rownames(b) <- colnames(x)
colnames(b) <- "β Coefficient Estimate"


# For all of the columns are in X, what does each element of β represent?

# For each element of β in combination with the columns of x (age, bmi, children, etc...),
# the element of β tells us how much that particular column in X contributes and influences the calculation of the predicted value of Y.
# In this case though, we are using the linear combinations of the columns describing the features of the individuals (age, bmi, children, etc...)
# along with the elements of β (to increase or decrease weight or influence) to calculate the predicted values of y or in this case the charges of health insurance.
# In otherwords, the linear combinations of the columns of x and the elements of β helps determine which features of the individuals has more or less influence on the predicted insurance charges for these individuals.


# ŷ = Xβ
predicted_y <- x %*% b

# ε = Y - Xβ = ŷ - y
e1 <- y - predicted_y
e2 <- y - x %*% b
print("error 1 vs error 2 equal ?: ")

[1] "error 1 vs error 2 equal ?: "

print(head(mean(e1)) == (head(mean(e2))))

[1] TRUE

# What is Y in this scenario?
# the Y dependent variable is the observed dependent response variable that we are trying to predict or estimate.
# In this case, the Y dependent variable is the cost of health insurance charges that is dependent on the variables/features of the individuals (age, sex, bmi, number of children, etc...)
# along with determining how the linear combinations of the coefficient elements (β) helps indicate and determine the impact/weight from which these individuals features of x (age, sex, bmi, number of children, etc...)
# actually has on specific predicted calculation costs of health insurance charges of these individuals.

# linear regression model : Y = Xβ + ε
linear_regression_model_y <- (x %*% b) + e1

# Show breakdown for first 5 Linear Regression Model values along with the predicted y and epison value ε
breakdown_of_linear_regression_model <- data.frame(
  Observed_Y_Linear_Regression_value = round(linear_regression_model_y, 2),
  Predicted_Y_value = round(predicted_y, 2),

# features/statistical measures that give different statistical aggregated measures about the data that lm() does not have.
# For example, ols() provides features/variables such as Lower 0.95 and Upper 0.95 that shows the lower and upper bonds of a 95% confidence interval
# along with Low and High features/variables that highlights the maximum and minimum values of the data along with a Diff = Difference feature that outlines
# the difference between the maximum and minimum values in the ols() outputs and finally it has a Type feature that highlights the type of the data.
# While the output of lm() only uniquely posseses the t value and Pr(>|t|) features/variables that highlights the p-value of a observation that is higher than the t-test statistic value and

# load Wage dataset
data(Wage)

# Task 1. Fit a linear model with age (and any relevant covariates,
# such as education or race) as predictors. 
# Create a partial effect plot of age and a component + residual plot (crPlots from the car package). 
# Does the relationship between age and wage look linear?

# Rename all the columns of the Wage dataset
colnames(Wage) <- c("year", "age", "marital_status", "race", "education", "region",
 "job_class", "health_status", "health_insurance", "log_wage", "wages")

# fitting a linear model with age and wage as predictors along with other relevant covariates such as education, etc...
lm_age <- lm(wages ~ age + education + race + job_class + marital_status, x = TRUE, y = TRUE, data = Wage)

# creating a partial effect plot and component + residual plots to visualize this relationship
crPlots(lm_age,
       main = "The Partial Effect Plot and a Component + Residual Plot: Wages by Age",
       ylab = "Wages ($)",
       col = "steelblue",
       pch = 16,
       lwd = 2.5,

# Does the relationship between age and wage look linear?

# From the partial effect plot and component + residual plots listed above, 
# it appears that the relationship between age and wage does not appear to be strictly linear and mostly non-linear. 
# In fact, the partial effect plot that shows the relationship between age and wages shows a large collection of data points that clusters together around the ages from 10-70 years old, but the clusters of the data
# are not smooth and linear. Instead, the shape of the data points distribution is more scattered and does not appear to follow a straight line, but 
# instead appears to show the data points being blotched together and randomly scattered in a way that does not follow a linear pattern. 
# The only thing to note is that the Purple (solid) line that represents the LOESS (locally weighted smoothing) in this plot shows that
# the data points do not appear to follow a linear pattern due to how the LOESS curve bends slightly downward and even deviated away from the 
# linear dottted Blue (dotted) line that represents the linear regression line implied by the fitted model to show the relationship of wages and ages along with the other 
# covariates (education, race, job class, marital status, etc..). From these details, it can be concluded that the relationship between age and wages is not linear along
# with the other covariates with being non-linear, not smooth and does not have good relationships between the wages and ages along with the covariates.


# Task 2. Fit a model replacing the linear age term with a 4-knot restricted cubic spline (rcs(age, 4)
# from the rms package). Create a partial effect plot of the RCS term. 
# How does the implied curve differ from the linear fit?

# Wage data being restructured to ensure that the column names are consistent with the previous code and to avoid any potential issues with column name mismatches.
dd <- datadist(Wage)
options(datadist = "dd")

# fitting a model replacing the linear age term with a 4-knot restricted cubic spline
rcs_4_cubic_spline_model <- ols(
  wages ~ rcs(age, 4) + education + race + job_class + marital_status,
  data = Wage, x = TRUE, y = TRUE
)

# Also fit linear version of the model from task 1 for comparison
linear_rms <- ols(
  wages ~ age + education + race + job_class + marital_status,
  data = Wage, x = TRUE, y = TRUE
)

# Partial effects (both models hold other covariates at datadist reference values)
p_spline <- Predict(rcs_4_cubic_spline_model, age, conf.int = FALSE)
p_linear <- Predict(linear_rms, age, conf.int = FALSE)

# Knot locations (for annotation)
knots <- rcspline.eval(Wage$age, nk = 4, knots.only = TRUE)
# Partial effects for the knot locations to at knots locations
p_knots <- Predict(rcs_4_cubic_spline_model, age = knots)

# plotting the partial effect plot
plot(p_spline$age, p_spline$yhat,
     type = "l", lwd = 2.8, col = "darkorange",
     xlab = "Age (years)", ylab = "Partial Effect on Wages ($)",
     main = "Partial Effect of Age on Wages\nLinear vs 4-Knot Restricted Cubic Spline Curve")

# Linear partial effect placed into the plot for comparison
lines(p_linear$age, p_linear$yhat, col = "blue", lwd = 2.2, lty = 2)

# Setting Knots linear lines
abline(v = knots, col = "red", lty = 3, lwd = 1.6)
# Add knot points
points(p_knots$age, p_knots$yhat, col = "red", pch = 19, cex = 1.3)
# Add knot labels
text(p_knots$age, p_knots$yhat, labels = round(knots, 1), col = "red", pos = 3, cex = 0.85)

# setting the legend to determine the curved and linear partial effect values along with the knot values in the plot
legend("topright",
       legend = c("RCS Partial Effect (4 knots)", 
                  "Linear Partial Effect", "Knot locations"),
       col = c("darkorange", "blue", "red"),
       lwd = c(2.8, 2.2, 1.6),
       lty = c(1, 2, 3),
       pch = c(NA, NA, 19),
       bty = "n", cex = 0.9)







# How does the implied curve differ from the linear fit?
# The implied curve from the 4-knot restricted cubic spline (RCS) model (orange line) differs from the linear fit model (blue line) in several ways.
# The Restricted Cubic Spline (RCS) model allows for more flexibility in capturing the relationship between age and wages, 
# resulting in a curve that can bend and adapt to the data. On top of that, the RCS model can capture non-linear patterns and 
# the knots at the specified locations in the model allows a slight rate of change and/or slope change to occur in the relationship 
# to where the data values in the relationship could experience a sudden exponential increase vertically, a slight increase and then decrease to create a small curve that signals
# a regression back to the previous linear relationship between the wages and ages, a sudden plateau where the data values in the relationship could experience a sudden decrease in a curved shape
# and finally another sudden decrease in values that will show the relationship of the ages and wages to be weaker than usual in the relationship.
# In comparision to the original linear fit relationship, which shows a constant linear and positive rate of change across all ages to wages. The only thing to note is that
# the linear fitted model tends to base its postive linear relationship on the data values that actually have a positive linear relationship between the ages and wages
# which is reflected in how all of the data values of the blue line starts around wages of 92.5 and goes up vertically as the line goes through all of the ages in comparison to the
# orange line which starts its wages measures around 72.5 and its line experience many changes (both vertically and horizontally) as the data line relationship goes through all of the ages values.
# Altogether, the RCS model provides a more nuanced and accurate representation of the relationship between age and wages along with the other covariates in the model in comparison to the linear fit model
# of task 1 which only reflects a somewhat artificial relationship between age and wages along with the covariates in the model.


# Task 3. Compute the cross-validated MSE for both models using k-fold cross-validation (following the approach from earlier modules)
#. Does the spline model offer a meaningful improvement in predictive accuracy?

total_rows_in_wages_data <- nrow(Wage)

# new restructuring of Wage data
dd <- datadist(Wage)
options(datadist = "dd")

# setting k-fold cross-validation k values
k_vals <- 20                            
folds <- folds <- sample(rep(1:k_vals, length.out = total_rows_in_wages_data))

# Function that computes MSE for one fold
compute_k_cross_validation_mse_value <- function(fold_num) {
  # setting the training and testing data
  train_data <- Wage[folds != fold_num, ]
  test_data  <- Wage[folds == fold_num, ]
  
  # Fit BOTH models on training data
  fit_lin <- ols(
    wages ~ age + education + race + job_class + marital_status,
    data = train_data, x = TRUE, y = TRUE
  )
  
  fit_rcs <- ols(
    wages ~ rcs(age, 4) + education + race + job_class + marital_status,
    data = train_data, x = TRUE, y = TRUE     # ← FIXED: now using train_data
  )
  
  # Predict on the TEST data
  pred_lin <- predict(fit_lin, newdata = test_data)
  pred_rcs <- predict(fit_rcs, newdata = test_data)
  # Return MSEs
  c(
    linear_mse = mean((pred_lin - test_data$wages)^2),
    rcs_mse    = mean((pred_rcs - test_data$wages)^2)
  )
}

# Apply the function to each fold
cv_results <- lapply(1:k_vals, compute_k_cross_validation_mse_value)

# Convert results to data frame and calculate averages
cv_results_df <- do.call(rbind, cv_results)

mean_cv_mse_linear <- mean(cv_results_df[, "linear_mse"])
mean_cv_mse_rcs    <- mean(cv_results_df[, "rcs_mse"])

cat("Linear Model CV MSE:   ", round(mean_cv_mse_linear, 2), "\n")

Linear Model CV MSE:    1237.91 

cat("RCS Spline Model CV MSE:", round(mean_cv_mse_rcs, 2), "\n")

RCS Spline Model CV MSE: 1212.41 

cat("Difference between models (RCS - Linear):", round(mean_cv_mse_rcs - mean_cv_mse_linear, 2), "\n")

Difference between models (RCS - Linear): -25.49 

cat("Percentage improvement:", round((mean_cv_mse_rcs - mean_cv_mse_linear) / mean_cv_mse_linear * 100, 2), "%\n")

Percentage improvement: -2.06 %

# Does the spline model offer a meaningful improvement in predictive accuracy in comparison with the linear model?

# From the calculations that we see from this code, it would be easy to interpret that the Spline or RCS model
# does not offer a meaningful improvement in predictive accuracy because the difference between the two models is 
# noticable even if it is noticably small. When the age and wage data from the Linear (training) model and RCS (testing) spline model is loaded into
# the compute_k_cross_validation_mse_value function along with the specified k value (1-20 in total), we can start to see that
# training data and testing data are very different and as they attempt to predict each other's y values with each iteration of k,
# they usually produce wage y values that are different from each other. In particular, the predicted y values from the RCS (testing) spline model and
# the Linear (training) model are always differing from each other regardless of the k value used in this cross valdidation process which in term 
# means that there is always a difference in the calculated MSE values between the two models. In this context though, the MSE Cross Validation value for
# the RCS (testing) spline model is much smaller than the MSE Cross Validation value for the linear model.
# As a result of these results, we can conclude the that the Spline or RCS model does not offer a meaningful improvement in predictive accuracy in comparison with the linear model,
# at least in the terms of believing that predictive accuracy is determined by the pureness of the linear line of data that these models are suppose to follow.
# From another context though, the Spline or RCS model does offer a more nuanced and complex predictive accuracy in terms of calculating a K-Fold Cross Validation MSE value
# that takes under the consideration all of the values and changes that comes with the covariates still left in the models when considering that a RCS model is able to capture non-linear behavior
# in its model in comparison to the linear model that only seems to be seeking out
# a pure linear relationship between the ages and wages along with the other covariates in the models.


#Task 4. Create a calibration plot for both models. Does either model show systematic 
# miscalibration across the predicted range?

# New Wage data being restructured to avoid column and row mismatches
dd <- datadist(Wage)
options(datadist = "dd")

# recreating Linear and RCS spline models for task 4

# fitting a model for 4-knot restricted cubic spline
rcs_spline_model <- ols(
  wages ~ rcs(age, 4) + education + race + job_class + marital_status,
  data = Wage, x = TRUE, y = TRUE
)

# fitting a model for being linear from task 1
linear_rms <- ols(
  wages ~ age + education + race + job_class + marital_status,
  data = Wage, x = TRUE, y = TRUE
)

# Predictions for partial effect plots for age
rcs_spline_prediction <- Predict(rcs_4_cubic_spline_model, age, conf.int = FALSE)
linear_prediction <- Predict(linear_rms, age, conf.int = FALSE)

calibration_of_training_testing_models_df <- data.frame(
  original_observed_values = Wage$wages,
  prediction_linear_values = linear_prediction$yhat,
  predictions_RCS_spline = rcs_spline_prediction$yhat
)

make_calib_plot <- function(df, pred_var, title) {
  ggplot(df, aes_string(x = pred_var, y = "original_observed_values")) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "gray25", size = 0.8) +
    geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1.2) +
    geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "black", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
    labs(
      title = title,
      subtitle = "Loess smooth (blue) vs. perfect calibration (dashed line)",
      x = "Predicted Wages ($)",
      y = "Observed Wages ($)"
    ) +
    theme_bw(base_size = 11) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey80", linewidth = 0.35),
      panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2),
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
      legend.position = "none"
    )
}



linear_model_calibration <- make_calib_plot(calibration_of_training_testing_models_df, "prediction_linear_values", "Calibration: Linear Model (Training Data Model)")

rcs_spline_model_calibration <- make_calib_plot(calibration_of_training_testing_models_df, "predictions_RCS_spline", "Calibration: 4-Knot RCS Spline Model (Testing Data Model)")

# miscalibration across the predicted range?
# After the creation of the calibration plots for both models and the data model that houses all of the data from the models in the code above,
# it would become clear to anyone that there is a huge systematic miscalibration across the predicted range for the linear model in comparison to the 4-knot restricted cubic spline model.
# This systematic miscalibration is expressed in the how the 4-knot restricted cubic spline model tends to have a line that bends and curves across all of the scattered data values/points of
# the entire distribution observed vs predicted wages data while the pure linear model only shows a pure linear relationship line between the predicted and observed wages data values. From these details,
# it would be clear to say that there is a miscalibration or misalignment in the conclusions of these two lines and both of the lines are attempting to tell a different story about the predicted vs observed
# values of wages. Upon some further evaluations of this data model and its other models, it then become clear to me that the closest accurate calibration of this data model would be the
# 4-knot restricted cubic spline model considering that the non-linear and non-normality relationship between the predicted and observed wages data shows a more nuanced and somewhat accurate
# representation of how the observed data is very close to the precticted wages data since the data across the whole data model is very scattered, shapeless and non-linear.
# Altogether though, this data model reveals a huge disconnect and an ability to show a good and accurate calibration plot between the predicted and observed values of wages
# regardless of the linear or 4-knot restricted cubic spline model used. In conclusion, there does not really seem to be huge systematic miscalibration in the data lines of these models at all, but instead 
# there does not seem to be any good calibration plot (regardless of the model) to show how the predicted values of wages are close to the observed values of wages and thus there is not good relationship between the predicted and observed values of wages.


#Task 5. State a final conclusion: should the nonlinear spline term be retained, 
# or is the linear model adequate? Address practical predictive improvement 
#(from MSE and calibration).

# In conclusion, the nonlinear 4-knot restricted cubic spline model/term should be retained and should be considered to be a somewhat good model to interpret
# the non-linear relationship between the predicted and observed values of wages along with interpreting that the wages and ages variables are connected in some way. 
# The linear model in this context would not be considered to be a good model to interpret any of the data within this context considering that the linear model is
# only seeking to seek out a pure linear relationship between the ages and wages along with the other covariates in the models and only seeks a linear
# relationship between the predicted and observed values of wages. In other words, the linear model is almost being mutually exclusive in seeking its conclusions and interpreations
# of the relationships within the data in a linear context and structure when it was clear that all of the data in the model was complex, nuanced and scattered from each other which signals
# the need for a non-linear relationship measure and formula to find the relationships in the data. The only thing of noteworthyness in terms of the practical improvements that
# could have been made to enforce this need of keeping the non-linear 4-knot restricted cubic spline model/term would be to improve the MSE score difference to be closer or greater in value
# than the linear model's MSE score in order to make the non-linear nature of the 4-knot restricted cubic spline model could be approximately normal or greater in value than the linear model.
# Then, the non-linear nature of the 4-knot restricted cubic spline model would be able to offer a more predictive accuracy improvement in the prediction of the observed values of wages to the
# predicted values of wages along with showing the positive relationships between the wages and the other covariates (such as ages, education, etc...).

# loading the riboflavin dataset
d1 <- readRDS("data/riboflavin.rds")
# d1 <- readRDS("/Users/Mzmz12/Documents/UVA Data Science Online Masters/DS6021_Predictability_Modeling/Module7_Linearmodel_≠_linear_relationship/riboflavin.rds")
# extracting the x and y values
x <- d1$x   # 71 × 4088 matrix. n = 71, p = 4088
y <- d1$y   # length-71 vector. n = 71
# extracting the column names
x_column_names <- colnames(x)
# setting the seed for reproducibility
set.seed(123)

#Problem 1. Confirm that OLS cannot create a unique solution for this dataset.
# Attempt to fit a linear model using lm() and report what happens.
# How many coefficients does R return as NA, and why?

# fitting the OLS model with y values to the x values will not work
#  due to how x <- d1$x has the dimensions of 71 × 4088 matrix. n = 71, p = 4088
# and p > n = 4088 > 71 violates the OLS requirements for a unique solution 
# ols_fitted_model <- ols(y ~ x)

# fitting the y values to the x values in a linear model
# this will work regardless of 𝑝 > 𝑛 --> rank(X) < p --> due to how d1$x has the dimensions of 71 × 4088 matrix. n = 71, p = 4088
# which is 4088 > 71 that agrees with the lm() requirements for a non-unique solution and
# due to the rank(x) < p which is 71 < 4088 which is true then lm() assures that there is a non-unique solution and that the output non-matching 
# coefficients from y to x will be NA

# fitting the y values to the x values in a linear model
linear_fitted_model <- lm(y ~ x)
# calculating the linear algebra rank
linear_rank <- qr(x)$rank


# Count how many coefficients are NA
number_of_na_coefficients_in_linear_model <- sum(is.na(linear_fitted_model$coefficients))
cat("Number of coefficients within the Linear Model combination of y to x that returns as NA:", number_of_na_coefficients_in_linear_model, "\n")

Number of coefficients within the Linear Model combination of y to x that returns as NA: 4018 

# The reason why we are getting NA is because the lm() requirements to have a unique solution are not met.
# This is evidenced through how this dataset has 71 observations  --> n = 71 and 4088 predictors --> p = 4088.
# Due to these n and p values, the lm() requirements to have a unique solution are not met and the full column rank
# of the design matrix X is not correct due to p > n = 4088 > 71. It is also important to note that the requirement
# of rank(x) < p being met due to 71 < 4088 that makes X^T * X singular and prevents OLS from having a unique solution.
# It is even important to realize that when the design matrix X is fitted to Y via lm(y ~ x), R uses QR decomposition and
# any predictor that is linearly dependent on others is dropped. The coefficients for these dropped predictors appear as NA.
# So, the NA values calculated here is only a result of the many dependent or non-ranked columns in this QR composition linear model
# fitting of y to x will automatically assign NA values to those columns values.


# Problem 2. Fit ridge regression using glmnet (alpha = 0). Select lambda λ
# via 10-fold cross-validation. Report λmin and λlse (LSE). Plot the Cross Valdidation (CV)
# curve with the two vertical lines. How does CV-MSE change as lambda λ increases?

# Ridge Regression: β(ridge) = arg β min ||Y - Xβ||^(2, 2) + λ ||β||^(2, 2)
# where λ > 0 is a tuning parameter (penalty) that controls the amount of shrinkage.
# The first term is the OLS loss where λ --> 0, then the ridge regression reduces the OLS loss.
# the second term penalizes the large coefficients of this regression line and as λ --> infinity,
# then this ridge regression gets closer to 0 (penalty).

# L2 penalty (ridge regression): ||x|| = x^t * x = √(x1 ^ 2 + x2 ^ 2 + ... + xd ^ 2)

# β^(LSE) ​= (X^T * X)^−1 * X^T * y
# X = design matrix of predictors,
# y = vector of observed responses,
# β^(LSE) = estimated regression coefficients.

# Fit ridge regression using glmnet (alpha = 0, fold cross-validation = 10).
cv_10_ridge <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0, nfolds = 10, type.measure = "mse")

# Plot the Cross Valdidation (CV) curve with the two vertical lines.
plot(cv_10_ridge, main = "Ridge Regression – 10-fold CV Error Curve")







# Getting the LSE and min lambda values
lambda_min_ridge <- cv_10_ridge$lambda.min
lambda_1se_ridge <- cv_10_ridge$lambda.1se

cat("Selected lambda (λ) (min CV-MSE):", round(lambda_min_ridge, 6), "\n")

Selected lambda (λ) (min CV-MSE): 5.934162 

cat("lambda.1se:", round(lambda_1se_ridge, 6), "\n\n")

lambda.1se: 36.41147 

# From the ridge regression plot that is produced from this code, we can witness how the Cross Valdidation (CV) Mean Squared Error (MSE)
# curve that describes the diference between this model's y values and this Riboflavin's y production values as a result of their relationship to their x values
# as the lambda λ increases or decreases along the -log(λ) x axis. In this case, the Cross Valdidation (CV) Mean Squared Error (MSE) curve is a downward sloping curve
# that is a result of the ridge regression model's ability to reduce the bias in the y values as the lambda λ increases. This decrease of bias is reflecting in how
# when the lambda λ is lower in value (such as -(log(λ=6))) then the bias of y is high due to the large difference in the MSE value at this point and how this MSE value
# difference seems to be getting smaller as the lambda λ gets higher in value (such as -(log(λ=1 or λ=0))) and thus the bias in the y values decreases. It is also important
# to note that this MSE value difference also reflects the changes in the variance of the y values as the lambda λ goes along the x axis. So, the variance of the MSE value difference
# is at its highest with the y value is biased at -(log(λ=6))) and tends to get smaller as the lambda λ decreases and moves along with x axis to get to the lowest variances at
# -(log(λ=1 or λ=0)). Finally, the penalty or shrinkage of the ridge regression model is reflected in how the MSE value difference is getting smaller as the lambda λ goes along the x axis.


# Problem 3. Plot the ridge coefficient path (
# on the x-axis, coefficient values on the y-axis). 
# Describe qualitatively what happens to the coefficients as 
# increases. Does any coefficient reach exactly zero?

plot(cv_10_ridge$glmnet.fit, 
     xvar = "lambda", 
     label = FALSE,
     main = "Ridge Coefficient Path (all coefficients shrink toward 0 to show when predicator variables don't effect the 𝑦 outcome)")







# L2 penalty (ridge regression): ||x|| = x^t * x = √(x1 ^ 2 + x2 ^ 2 + ... + xd ^ 2)

# From this ridge regression plot and its coefficient pathway on the y axis,
# we can see how the 𝛽 coefficients values are going from smaller (𝛽 = 0.0 --> no effect on the predicted y outcomes) to the higher
# (𝛽 = 0.02 --> some to a lot of effect on the predicted y outcomes) as the lambda λ goes along the x axis. In this case, the coefficient values are getting
# bigger and you can tell from the lines in the plot that the predict y value outcomes are heavily influenced by 𝛽 coefficient as we move along the lambda λ values along the x axis.
# Thus, the coefficient values are getting quantitatively bigger and the variances of these predicted y values that derives from the linear combinations of these increasing coefficients
# to their x predictor variables are along getting quantitatively bigger and more diverse as we move along the lambda λ x axis values.
# The only two things to note in this case, is that the coefficient of these predicted y values and all of these data lines actually starts at 0.0 when the lambda λ value is at its highest
# -log(λ > -6). The second thing is that the coefficient values of this plot seem to be kept within a small range of values due to 
# the penalties and loss functions that are being used to keep the coefficients from getting too large and imposes the L2 penalty on the ridge regression model to keep the coefficent pathways smooth as it goes from zero to larger values.
# As reflected by this equation, Loss = min𝛽 * ||y - x𝛽||^2 + 𝜆 ∑ 𝛽^2j


# Problem 4. Fit lasso regression (alpha = 1) and select λ
# via 10-fold CV. At λlse,
# how many gene expression features are retained 
# (non-zero coefficients)? Plot the coefficient path and the 
# CV error curve.

# Fit lasso regression
cv_lasso <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1, nfolds = 10, type.measure = "mse")

# Getting the LSE and min lambda values
lambda_1se_lasso <- cv_10_ridge$lambda.1se
lambda_min_lasso <- cv_lasso$lambda.min

# Number of non-zero gene expression features at lambda.lse
coef_lasso <- coef(cv_lasso, s = "lambda.1se")
fianl_num_retained_lasso <- sum(coef_lasso[-1, ] != 0)

# Print the Lambda 
cat("lambda.1se (lasso):", round(lambda_1se_lasso, 6), "\n")

lambda.1se (lasso): 36.41147 

cat("Number of retained (non-zero) gene expression features at lambda.lse:", 
    fianl_num_retained_lasso, "\n\n")

Number of retained (non-zero) gene expression features at lambda.lse: 25 

#Plot CV error curve with the two vertical lines
plot(cv_lasso, main = "Lasso Regression – 10-fold CV Error Curve")







# Coefficient path plot
plot(cv_lasso$glmnet.fit, 
     xvar = "lambda", 
     main = "Lasso Coefficient Path (many coefficients hit exactly zero)")







# Problem 5. Fit an elastic net with α ϵ {0.25, 0.5, 0.75}.
# For each α, record the minimum CV-MSE. 
# Create a small table showing (α, λmin, min CV-MSE).
# Which α yields the lowest CV-MSE? 
# How does the number of selected features compare to lasso?

# Setting the alpha, min lambda, min CV-MSE, and number of selected elastic net features
alphas <- seq(0.25, 0.75, by = 0.25)
min_cv_mse_elastic_net <- numeric(length(alphas))
lambda_min_elastic_net <- numeric(length(alphas))
num_selected_elastic_net <- numeric(length(alphas))

# Fitting elastic net through looping and appending the values to the vectors
for (i in seq_along(alphas)) {
  cv_elastic_net <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = alphas[i], nfolds = 10, type.measure = "mse")
  min_cv_mse_elastic_net[i] <- min(cv_elastic_net$cvm)
  lambda_min_elastic_net[i] <- cv_elastic_net$lambda.min
  coefs <- coef(cv_elastic_net, s = cv_elastic_net$lambda.min)
  num_selected_elastic_net[i] <- sum(coefs[-1, ] != 0)
}

# creating a final data frame table
results_elastic_net <- data.frame(
  alpha = alphas,
  min_lambda = round(lambda_min_elastic_net, 6),
  min_CV_MSE = round(min_cv_mse_elastic_net, 6),
  num_nonzero_features = num_selected_elastic_net
)

# printing the results and comparing the number of selected features
print(results_elastic_net)

  alpha min_lambda min_CV_MSE num_nonzero_features

for(i in seq_along(num_selected_elastic_net)){
  cat(" The Elastic Net (alpha=", alphas[i], " and selected ", num_selected_elastic_net[i], " features.\n")
  cat(" The Lasso (alpha=1) selected", fianl_num_retained_lasso, "features.\n")

# From this data frame of all (α = alphas, λmin = lambda mins, min CV-MSE = minimum cross validation mean squared error, and num_nonzero_features = number of nonzero features extracted from the lambda min values),
# it would be easy to witness that the alpha that yields the lowest CV-MSE is the alpha of 0.25 where the lowest CV-MSE value of 0.203687.

# Problem 6. Fit PCR using the pls package (or manual SVD as shown in the chapter). Select the number of 
# components k-fold value(s) via cross-validation (use validationplot). Report the optimal k.

# fitting PCR model with the y and x variable values and the k-fold value is 10
pcr_fit <- pcr(y ~ x, scale = TRUE, validation = "CV", segments = 10)

# Validation plot – choose optimal number of components
validationplot(pcr_fit, val.type = "MSE", 
               main = "PCR: k=10 fold - Cross-Validation Error vs Number of PC Components",
               xlab = "Number of Principal Components (PC)", ylab = "Cross-Validation Mean-Squared Error (MSE)",
               legendpos = "topright")







# Identify optimal k
optimal_minimum_PLS_k_val <- pcr_fit$validation$PRESS[which.min(pcr_fit$validation$PRESS)] - 1
cat("The Optimal k number of the minimum number of PC components in the calcualted Partial Least Square Regression Value (PLS):", optimal_minimum_PLS_k_val, "\n\n")

The Optimal k number of the minimum number of PC components in the calcualted Partial Least Square Regression Value (PLS): 16.62265 

#  a. Back-transform the PCR fit to obtain the full 4,088-dimensional coefficient vector 𝛽pcr.
# transforming 

# this performs a backdoor linear transformation on all x values with the coefficients altered by the most optimal k number of principal components value for the PCR fitted object
backdoor_coefficient_transformation_on_k_optimal_pcr <- coef(pcr_fit, ncomp = optimal_minimum_PLS_k_val)
dimensions <- dim(backdoor_coefficient_transformation_on_k_optimal_pcr)[0:1]
cat("Checking all of the Dimensions in the PCR object to check if the transformations of the coefficients in the coefficient vector is correct for 4088: ", dimensions, "\n\n")

#  b. Use 𝛽 pcr to generate a prediction for the first observation in the dataset (X[1, ]). Show your R code and the predicted value.
pred_first_observation_of_the_pcr <- predict(pcr_fit, 
                      newdata = x[1, , drop = FALSE],

# predicted value for the first observation

#  c. In one sentence, explain why you needed all 4,088 gene expression values to generate
# this prediction even though you only fit k principal components.

# For any prection to be accurate, we need to use all 4088 genes in linear combination with 
# the new principal components (uncorrelated representations of the original predictor variables x) 
# along with the coefficients in the coefficent vector to generate the accurate full-wieghted predictions of all 
# the 4088 genes in this data set.

# Problem 7. Comparing across methods:

  # a. Report the CV-MSE at the selected tuning parameter for each of the four methods (ridge, lasso, elastic net, PCR).
min_cv_mse_ridge <- min(cv_10_ridge$cvm)
min_cv_mse_lasso <- min(cv_lasso$cvm)

  # b. Which method achieves the lowest CV-MSE on this dataset?

# Compare the lowest CV-MSE values for all methods
lowest_cv_mse_methods <- c("Ridge Regression", "Lasso Regression", "Elastic Net Regression", "Partial Least Square Regression")
lowest_cv_mse_values <- c(min_cv_mse_ridge, min_cv_mse_lasso, min_cv_mse_elastic_net, cv_mse_pcr)

# Find the method with the lowest CV-MSE
lowest_cv_mse_index <- which.min(lowest_cv_mse_values)
lowest_cv_mse_method_val <- lowest_cv_mse_methods[lowest_cv_mse_index]

# Print the results
cat("The lowest CV-MSE achieved by the methods are:\n")

The lowest CV-MSE achieved by the methods are:

for (i in 1:length(lowest_cv_mse_methods)) {
  cat(lowest_cv_mse_methods[i], ": ", lowest_cv_mse_values[i], "\n")
}

Lasso Regression :  0.2096233 
Elastic Net Regression :  0.2036869 
Partial Least Square Regression :  0.2482063 

# Print the method with the lowest CV-MSE
cat("\nThe method with the lowest CV-MSE is:", lowest_cv_mse_method_val)


The method with the lowest CV-MSE is: Elastic Net Regression

  # c. Based on the guidance in the chapter, would you expect ridge or lasso to work better here?
  # Does the data agree with your expectation? Why or why not?

  # Due to all of the details and guidance within this chapter 7., I would say that since this data set is 
  # a high-dimensional genetics dataset that would need to have some kind of correctlation, association and
  # even collinearity within the features/variables in the dataset since the calculation of the CV-MSE values
  # needs to be accurate considering that this measure is attempto to determine which method(s) actually predicts acccurately
  # to the already observed values/data in the genetics dataset along with being able to predict any changes in the prediction model 
  # that would occur such as the inputting of new data and how these predictions reacts to changing values across the x axis such as 
  # how the lambda λ values in the ridge regression and lasso regression models. It is also important to note that these
  # MSE values are also dependent on the lambda λ values that are used in the ridge regression and lasso regression models 
  # along with helping determine the bias in the y prediction values and how the variances that these MSE help define behave and/or are structured
  # as the lambda λ values increase or decrease. In this case though, the data here really does meet my expectation in accurately predicting
  # the predicted y values from how these MSE values from each model reveals in the most non-linear way and the non-consideration of intercept and zero values
  # which model is more accurate or not based on its lower CV-MSE value. In conclusion after some comparisons between the ridge and lassoo prediction models
  # along with the the MSE curve values calculated in this chapter (Ridge Regression :  0.2694971, Lasso Regression :  0.2096233, Elastic Net Regression :  0.2036869 )
  # it becomes easy to conclude that the Lasso Regression (and possibly the Elastic Net Regression) is more accurate in predicting the y values in this genetics dataset.
  # This conclusion is backed up by the MSE values listed above.
